skill.name: firebase-development
skill.description: Guides Firebase project setup, feature development, debugging, and validation using TypeScript, emulators, Firestore rules, Cloud Functions, and testing workflows.
skill.summary: Build, debug, and review Firebase projects with emulator-first TypeScript workflows.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows most are Markdown examples, inline code formatting, local Firebase emulator URLs, and normal developer commands. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found. The skill still carries medium risk because it instructs assistants to run shell commands and inspect environment files during Firebase debugging.

medium_findings.title: Environment File Inspection Guidance
medium_findings.description: The debug workflow instructs the assistant to run commands that print `functions/.env` and `hosting/.env.local`. This is legitimate Firebase troubleshooting, but it can expose API keys, tokens, or service configuration into the assistant context if users do not redact secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact commands are present in a debugging step and target environment files. The context is legitimate, but the credential exposure risk is direct.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Execution Workflow
medium_findings.description: Multiple workflows direct the assistant to execute Firebase CLI, npm, git, lsof, kill, grep, and file inspection commands. These are normal development tasks, but they can modify repositories, terminate processes, or reveal local project information if run without confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and semantically tied to setup, debugging, and validation. They are not malicious, but they create real operational risk in user workspaces.

low_findings.title: Localhost URL and IP Findings Are Benign
low_findings.description: The hardcoded network findings point to `http://127.0.0.1:4000`, the Firebase Emulator UI. This is a local development URL, not an external endpoint or data exfiltration target.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every reviewed URL is localhost emulator guidance. No evidence found of remote network exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Findings Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer classified Markdown inline code and fenced examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The files are Markdown instructions and do not contain executable Ruby backtick operators.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious syntax appears inside Markdown code spans or code fences. Some commands may be run by an assistant, but the static Ruby execution classification is incorrect.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Weak Crypto Findings Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The flagged path traversal and weak cryptographic algorithm locations correspond to TypeScript relative imports, metadata, API key wording, or security review guidance. No evidence found of traversal logic, cryptographic implementation, or unsafe hash use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found normal Firebase examples rather than security-sensitive file access or crypto code. The scanner appears to match generic tokens without semantic context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Potential Secret Disclosure Through Environment File Reads
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands that print `.env` files can disclose secrets. Users should redact values before the assistant reads or quotes these files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This pattern is explicit and directly affects secret handling. The workflow intent is debugging, not exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Workspace-Modifying Command Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: Setup instructions include project creation, git initialization, commits, Firebase initialization, dependency installation guidance, and emulator commands. These are expected for Firebase development but should run only in the intended workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are operational and may alter local files or repository state. Their intent is normal project setup.

dangerous_patterns.title: Process Termination Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The debug workflow recommends `kill -9 <PID>` for port conflicts. This can terminate unrelated local processes if the PID is selected incorrectly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and potentially disruptive. It is a common debugging action but requires user confirmation and process verification.

content.value_statement: Firebase projects can become hard to structure, test, and secure as features grow. This skill guides setup, feature work, debugging, and validation with clear emulator-first workflows.
actual_capabilities: Routes Firebase requests to setup, feature, debug, or validation workflows.
Guides Firebase CLI setup, hosting choices, emulator configuration, and initial project structure.
Provides test-first steps for adding Cloud Functions, endpoints, and Firestore collections.
Troubleshoots emulator, Firestore rules, authentication, function, and deployment issues.
Reviews Firebase configuration, Firestore rules, authentication patterns, tests, and production readiness.
Promotes TypeScript, Vitest, Biome, ABOUTME comments, and consistent handler responses.
limitations: It is guidance only and depends on the assistant executing commands in the user workspace.
It references local production project paths that may not exist for other users.
It does not include the referenced docs/examples files in this package.
It may ask to inspect environment files during debugging, so users should redact secrets first.

use_cases.title: Initialize a Firebase App
use_cases.description: Set up Firebase hosting, functions, Firestore, emulators, TypeScript, tests, and project structure from a new repository.

use_cases.title: Add a Secure Feature
use_cases.description: Create a Cloud Function, API endpoint, or Firestore collection with tests, authentication checks, rules, and emulator verification.

use_cases.title: Review Before Release
use_cases.description: Check Firebase configuration, rules, authentication, test coverage, and deployment readiness before production changes.

prompt_templates.title: Start a New Firebase Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the firebase-development skill to set up a new Firebase project with Firestore, Cloud Functions, Hosting, TypeScript, and emulators.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Firestore Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the firebase-development add-feature workflow to add a new Firestore collection with rules, tests, and emulator verification.

prompt_templates.title: Debug an Emulator Problem
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the firebase-development debug workflow to investigate why my Firebase emulators fail to start or return permission errors.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the firebase-development validate workflow to review my Firebase project for security rules, auth checks, testing, and deployment readiness.

output_examples.input: Set up a Firebase app for a small product dashboard.
output: A setup checklist with Firebase CLI, hosting, functions, Firestore, and emulator steps.
Architecture choices for hosting, authentication, functions, and Firestore write model.
Verification steps for build, tests, linting, emulator startup, and environment files.

output_examples.input: Add an authenticated endpoint that writes to Firestore.
output: A test-first implementation plan for the new endpoint.
Security rule guidance for server-write-only or client-write patterns.
Emulator checks for response behavior, documents, authentication, and rules.

output_examples.input: Review my Firebase project before deployment.
output: A categorized review of configuration, Firestore rules, function architecture, authentication, and tests.
A list of critical, important, and nice-to-have remediation items.
Production readiness checks for audit status, build status, tests, project selection, and indexes.

best_practices: Run Firebase workflows in a clean project workspace and review command effects before execution.
Use emulators and tests before deploying functions, rules, hosting, or authentication changes.
Redact secrets before sharing `.env`, debug logs, or Firebase configuration with any assistant.
anti_patterns: Do not run setup or debug commands in the wrong repository or Firebase project.
Do not expose production API keys, service credentials, or environment files in chat context.
Do not deploy before emulator tests, Firestore rules checks, and build validation pass.

faq.question: What Firebase tasks does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports setup, feature development, debugging, and validation for Firebase projects using Cloud Functions, Firestore, Hosting, and emulators.

faq.question: Does it create production Firebase resources automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides steps that may use Firebase CLI commands. Users should confirm project targets before running deployment commands.

faq.question: Can it help with Firestore security rules?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides server-write-only and client-write validation patterns and recommends default deny rules.

faq.question: Does it require local Firebase tools?
faq.answer: Most workflows assume Firebase CLI, Node.js tooling, TypeScript, and local emulators are available.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with secret files?
faq.answer: Use caution. The debug workflow can inspect environment files, so redact secrets before sharing file contents.

faq.question: Can it review existing Firebase code?
faq.answer: Yes. The validation workflow checks configuration, authentication, rules, tests, architecture consistency, and production readiness.
